coredata.dc:title: A molecular imprinted fluorescence sensor based on carbon quantum dots for selective detection of 4-nitrophenol in aqueous environments
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Kunpeng
Tan, Liju
Zhang, Yuewei
Zhang, Dongmei
Wang, Na
Wang, Jiangtao
coredata.dc:description: P-nitrophenol (4-NP) is the most persistent and highly toxic species among nitrophenol. In this work, a novel fluorescent probe for the detection of 4-NP in aqueous environment was constructed by combining the carbon dots (CQDs) with excellent optical properties and the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) with favorable selectivity. The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid hydrate as carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as surface modifier, then the molecularly imprinted polymers coating on the CQDs (MIP@CQDs) were obtained by sol-gel imprinting process. The fluorescence quenching of MIP@CQDs is the results of internal filtration effect and dynamic quenching when they encounter with 4-NP. The probe is suitable for the quantitative detection of trace 4-NP in actual aqueous samples, such as tap water, wastewater and seawater, with satisfying recoveries from 95.1 % to 107.8 %, wide detection linear ranges between 0 and 144 μmol/L, low detection limit of 0.41 μmol/L and high selectivity. The detection results are consistent with those of the HPLC method. This work provides a simple, rapid and effective fluorescent detection method for trace 4-NP in aqueous environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Molecular imprinted
P-nitrophenol
Fluorescence quenching
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of MIP@CQDs sensor synthesis conditions and fluorescence conditions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Marine Ecological Environment Science and Engineering, Ministry of Natural Resources
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41876078 ) and the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Marine Ecological Environment Science and Engineering, Ministry of Natural Resources (No. MESE-2019-06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of MIP@CQDs (a), CQDs (b) and NIP@CQDs (c).
FTIR spectrum of NIP@CQDs and MIP@CQDs after washing the template.
A co-plot showing UV-absorption spectrum (UV), excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of MIP@CQDs.
The effects of changing the ratio of functional monomers, template molecule and crosslinking agent (A) and pH (B) on fluorescence quenching efficiency in presence of 4-NP.
The quenching efficiency (F0/F) of MIP@CQDs and NIP@CQDs by different concentrations of 4-NP (A), and plots of Ln (F0/F) of MIP@CQDs versus concentrations of 4-NP (B).

The fluorescence quenching efficiency of 4-NP (36 μmol/L) and analogue (36 μmol/L) on MIP@CQDs and NIP@CQDs (A), and the fluorescence quenching efficiency of 4-NP (36 μmol/L) and common inorganic ions (36 μmol/L) on MIP@CQDs and NIP@CQDs (B). All experiments were performed in pH 8.5 buffer.

A co-plot of the UV-absorption spectra of 4-NP and the excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of MIP@CQDs (A). Suppressed efficiency of observed and corrected fluorescence of MIP@CQDs after addition of different concentrations of 4-NP (B). The Stern-Volmer plots of 4-NP for concentrations up to 144 μmol/L at different temperature (C).
Illustration of the synthetic process of MIP@CQDs as 4-NP sensing.
Infographic illustration of the main steps to elucidate the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching of MIP@CQDs by 4-NP.
Parameters of the Stern-Volmer relationship of 4-NP with MIP@CQDs at different temperature.

head.ce:title: A molecular imprinted fluorescence sensor based on carbon quantum dots for selective detection of 4-nitrophenol in aqueous environments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: J. Wang, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: P-nitrophenol (4-NP) is the most persistent and highly toxic species among nitrophenol. In this work, a novel fluorescent probe for the detection of 4-NP in aqueous environment was constructed by combining the carbon dots (CQDs) with excellent optical properties and the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) with favorable selectivity. The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid hydrate as carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as surface modifier, then the molecularly imprinted polymers coating on the CQDs (MIP@CQDs) were obtained by sol-gel imprinting process. The fluorescence quenching of MIP@CQDs is the results of internal filtration effect and dynamic quenching when they encounter with 4-NP. The probe is suitable for the quantitative detection of trace 4-NP in actual aqueous samples, such as tap water, wastewater and seawater, with satisfying recoveries from 95.1 % to 107.8 %, wide detection linear ranges between 0 and 144 μmol/L, low detection limit of 0.41 μmol/L and high selectivity. The detection results are consistent with those of the HPLC method. This work provides a simple, rapid and effective fluorescent detection method for trace 4-NP in aqueous environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A large number of toxic organic pollutants in the wastewater produced by industry are inevitably discharged into the environment with the progress of society and the development of industry. Nitrophenol is one of the most abundant toxicants among pollutants in natural environments and is difficult to be determined due to its low concentration (Tang et al., 2013). There are three isoforms of nitrophenol (Dinda et al., 2014), and p-nitrophenol (4-NP) is the most persistent and highly toxic nitrophenol (Shu et al., 2018). 4-NP is usually used as an intermediate in the production of synthetic dyes, leather processing and drug production, etc. (Huang et al., 2019a), for example, as an intermediate in the synthesis of pharmaceutical compounds such as paracetamphenol and phenacetin, pesticides such as parathion and phosphorus salon, and azo dyes (El-Shaheny et al., 2020). 4-NP is regarded as a priority pollutant in industrial and agricultural wastewaters and discharged into the environment with wastewaters. Low concentration of 4-NP may cause a large number of aquatic organisms to die, inhibit microbial communities, and lead to cancer for animals (Liu et al., 2014). At the same time, it also causes acute poisoning and coma death to humans (Qiu et al., 2018). Depending on its stability, water solubility and resistance to degradation, 4-NP can exist in the environment for a long time (Jiang et al., 2016). Due to its harmful effects, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) set its maximum allowable level as 0.43 μmol/L in drinking water (Granary and Sakthivel, 2011). Therefore, establishing an accurate and sensitive method to detect 4-NP is of great significance and urgent.

There are various methods that can be used to detect 4-NP at present, such as spectrophotometry (Niazi and Yazdanipour, 2007), electrochemical method (Pandith et al., 2016), chromatography technology (Sukhanov et al., 2017), capillary electrophoresis (Huo et al., 2011), chemiluminescence method (Chen et al., 2016), fluorescence method (Ren et al., 2018), etc. However, in the process of detecting trace 4-NP, some of the above methods cannot be put into practical application due to the complex use of instruments, requirement of a large number of samples, complex and tedious separation and pretreatment procedures (Yu et al., 2017). As a result, the experimental process was time-consuming and expensive, and even toxic substances and secondary pollutants might be produced (Tian et al., 2019; Hao et al., 2016a). Fluorescence spectrophotometry is widely used in the analysis and detection of substances due to its simple operation, good selectivity and sensitivity, and low use cost (Shi et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2019). Han and his colleagues developed a reversible fluorescent nanoswitch for visual and selective detection of 4-NP in ambient and drinking water by changing pH of the system (Han et al., 2019). Xu et al. (2022) designed a molecularly imprinted polydopamine coating CdTe@SiO2 as a ratio fluorescence probe for ultra-fast and visual 4-NP detection. Most of fluorescence methods were involved in complicated synthesis, slow response and low resolution for 4-NP, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), which limited the application of these methods. In addition, many of these technologies rely on the use of metal-based nanomaterials (Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2018; Mao et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015), which are highly potentially cytotoxic (Pacheco and Buzea, 2017). For the sake of developing a convenient, rapid and effective method for detecting 4-NP, non-toxic, safe and blue luminescent primitive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) combined with molecular imprinting technology are applied (MIP@CQDs). In this paper, the quantum dots were modified by molecular blotting technique to retain their fluorescence properties and increase their selective specificity. The strong fluorescence of MIP@CQDs at 441 nm (λex 365 nm) was effectively quenched in the presence of 4-NP and the quenching mechanism was further explored. 4-NP in tap water, wastewater and seawater was then determined and compared with the results of HPLC method. It was indicated that the MIP@CQDs sensor can be successfully applied to determine 4-NP in actual water.

The morphology of MIP@CQDs and CQDs was characterized by JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was taken on Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific Corporation). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2500 spectrophotometer (Japan). A HPLC (L2000, Hitachi Japan) with C18 reversed-phase column were used as a comparison of determined data. Fluorescence spectra was recorded on F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a standard 10 mm path length colorimetric quartz tube. CenLee16R table top high speed centrifuge (China) was used for centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 rpm. FD-1A-5D freeze dryer (China) was used for freeze drying. All Milli-Q water was obtained from CLW-K10-WP-C Milli-Q water machine (China).

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), o-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), p-nitrophenol (4-NP), p-nitrophenol (standard, 500 μg/mL), hydroquinone (HQ), phloroglucinol (PG), 4-nitrotoluene (4-NT), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), p-phenylenediamine (p-PD), o-nitrophenol (2-NP), m-nitrophenol (3-NP), aniline (AN), nitrobenzene(NB), 2,4-dichlorophenol (C6H4Cl2O) and hydroquinone (C6H6O2) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). 3-Aminopropyl trimethoxy silane (APTMS), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonia (ammonium hydroxide), ethanol (anhydrous ethanol), methanol, sodium chloride (NaCl), anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), ammonium acetate buffer solution, anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O) were supplied by Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Milli-Q water was used through all experiments.

CQDs was prepared from CA (citric acid monohydrate) and O-PDA (o-phenylenediamine) by one-pot method. 2 mmol CA and 4 mmol o-PDA were dissolved in 20 mL Milli-Q water at 60 °C with stirring. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particles in the supernatant. After that, the obtained CQDs suspension was dialyzed with Milli-Q water in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 12 h to remove unreacted small molecules. Finally, the CQDs were freeze-dried and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

The molecular imprinted polymer MIP@CQDs was prepared by sol-gel method. 4-NP was used as the template molecule, 3-aminopropyl trimethoxy silane (APTMS) as the functional monomer, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as the cross-linking agent, and concentrated ammonia (NH3·H2O) as the catalyst. 200 mg of freeze-dried solid CQDs was dispersed in 20 mL Milli-Q water as store for future use. 0.05 mmol of 4-NP and 0.2 mmol of APTMS were dissolved in 15 mL ethanol and stirred for 30 min using magnetic stirrer, then 1 mmol of TEOS was added and stirred for 10 min. 0.3 mL of concentrated ammonia and 0.5 mL of CQDs were added to the reaction flask and stirred for 12 h. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to collect the precipitate. The samples were washed three times with ethanol. Finally, the collected MIP@CQDs was freeze-dried under vacuum. In addition, non-molecularly imprinted polymers coating on the CQDs (NIP@CQDs) were prepared as the same procedure without adding 4-NP to compare the blotting ability with MIP@CQDs. The whole synthesis process was shown in Scheme 1 .

The determination of 4-NP based on MIP@CQDs sensor was carried out according to the following steps. Firstly, 0.0200 g of MIP@CQDs was prepared in 20 mL ultrapure water as a stock solution with a concentration of 1 g/L, and a series of standard solutions with a concentration of 4-NP were also prepared. Then 1 mL of MIP@CQDs solution and 4-NP standard solution were added into 10 mL centrifuge tube diluted with borate buffer solution (pH = 8.5) and incubated for 1 min at 25 °C to determine fluorescence. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solution were determined at the 441 nm under excitation of 365 nm. The fluorescence quenching variable (Ln (F0/F)) had a good linear relationship with the concentration of 4-NP, which was better than the relationship between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) and 4-NP concentration. Among them, F0 and F refer to the maximum value of the fluorescence before and after the addition of 4-NP to the MIP@CQDs solution, respectively. All the fluorescence detections above were tested under room temperature, and similar tests were compared under the same testing conditions.

Tap water was obtained from Ocean University of China. Seawater was sampled from nearshore of Qingdao city. The wastewater came from the river in Licang District, Qingdao, near the chemical plant. Each 300 mL of water sample was extracted twice through 20 mL of dichloromethane and then filtered through 0.2 μm Nylon membrane, and the filtrate was nitrogen-blown to dryness and redissolved in 10 mL of Milli-Q water to achieve 30-fold enrichment (Li and Li, 2011). The samples were spiked by different concentrations of 4-NP (0 μmol/L, 5.4 μmol/L, 10.8 μmol/L and 18 μmol/L) with a standard solution. The fluorescence emission spectra under 365 nm excitation were recorded. Each result is the average of three replicates.

In order to explore the properties of the fluorescence sensor, the structure of the material was characterized. The prepared MIP@CQDs, CQDs and NIP@CQDs were scanned by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as shown in Fig. 1 . The CQDs are uniform and microspherical particles with the size about 5 nm. The MIP@CQDs and NIP@CQDs are spherical structures of similar shape with an increase in particle size of about 20 nm, indicating the presence of a core-shell structure in the material (Fang et al., 2019).

In order to compare the composition of molecularly imprinted polymers, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on NIP@CQDs and MIP@CQDs after washing the template as shown in Fig. 2 . According to Fig. 2, MIP@CQDs and NIP@CQDs exhibited similar spectra, indicating the similar chemical structures. Both of them have characteristic peaks at 433 cm−1, 784 cm−1 and 1041 cm−1 (Chen et al., 2019), corresponding to SiOSi bending vibration, SiOSi symmetric stretching vibration and SiOSi asymmetric stretching vibration (Romero et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018), which indicates the effective formation of SiO2 shell layer. The characteristic peaks at 1545 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibrations of NH, indicating that –NH2 of APTES is attached to the surface of the SiO2 layer. All these indicated the successful synthesis of MIPs coating on the quantum dots (Paul et al., 2016).

The optical characters of the MIP@CQDs were elucidated by UV–VIS and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV spectrum (Fig. 3 ) of MIP@CQDs shows two peaks at 235 and 370 nm assigning π-π* transition of CC conjugated system (core band) and n-π* transition of CO (edge band), respectively. The MIP@CQDs has an intense blue fluorescence with maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 365/441 nm (Fig. 3).

MIP@CQDs solution was stored at room temperature for 10 days to study its optical stability. As depicted in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of MIP@CQDs showed no significant difference in 10 days, displaying satisfactory photostability (Carbonaro et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of MIP@CQDs sensor synthesis conditions and fluorescence conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The conditions for the synthesis of MIP@CQDs sensors as well as the fluorescence conditions were optimized, mainly including the ratio of template molecule to functional monomer and crosslinking agent, solution acidity, MIP@CQDs dosage and response time. The quenching efficiency was defined as F0/F as an indicator of quenching ability. The effect of different ratios among template molecule, functional monomer and cross-linker on quenching efficiency was shown in Fig. 4A. The synthesis and function of polymer are affected by the proportion of functional monomer, cross-linking agent and template molecule, which directly affects the detection performance of 4-NP by the fluorescence sensor. In the synthesis processes of MIP@CQDs, the amounts of template molecule, functional monomer and cross-linker were designed around the ratio of 1:4:20 according to Hao et al. (2016b) and Yang et al. (2018) by changing one of the concentrations through the controlled variable method. It was finally found that the ratio of 1:4:20 is the best for fluorescence quenching assay. Too much monomer will increase non-specific adsorption and decrease selectivity. Thin imprinting layer makes the adsorption site insufficient, which has a great influence on the adsorption capacity, however, thick imprinted layer makes the template molecules embedded too deeply, which makes it difficult to be adsorbed and desorbed for the target molecules (Hao et al., 2016b; Lin et al., 2016).

The quenching efficiency of 4-NP on MIP@CQDs is affected by pH and the fluorescence changes of MIP@CQDs with pH were compared as shown in Fig. 4B. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of MIP@CQDs is low at low pH, and increases rapidly when pH is higher than 6, and stabilizes at pH 10 from Fig. 4B. The over-acid condition has an inhibitory effect on the fluorescence sensor, which may change the structure of the imprinted cavity that specifically recognize 4-NP. Considering the practical application, the subsequent experiments were carried out under pH 8.5.

The effects of MIP@CQDs dosage as well as incubation time were also tested. The values of F0/F were measured under different dosage and incubation time for 36 μmol/L 4-NP as shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3. The amounts of MIP@CQDs affect the quenching efficiency. A small amount of MIP@CQDs cause it not to adsorb enough analyte, and the fluorescence intensity of small amount MIP@CQDs is also weak, both which result in a large error in determination. Excessive MIP@CQDs not only cause waste, but also produce fluorescence self-absorption, thus affecting the accuracy of detection. The quenching efficiency increases with the dosage of MIP@CQDs and reaches the maximum at 1 g/L−1, and the quenching efficiency starts to decrease with further increase in dosage according to Fig. S2. The response time is also an important condition for fluorescence detection, which affects the detection efficiency. As shown in Fig. S3 the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly after the addition of 4-NP and reached minimum after 1 min. Therefore, the complete reaction can be reached at 1 min, which is the basis for achieving rapid detection and analysis. Accordingly, 1 min was chosen as the response time in further experiments for the determination of 4-NP.

After optimizing the conditions, the MIP@CQDs were synthesized and washed to remove the template for further use. The supernatant after each ethanol washing was scanned by UV spectrophotometer and its absorption value was measured as shown in Fig. S4. The UV absorption value at the wavelength of 400 nm gradually decreased with the increase of washing times, and approached to zero after three times of ethanol washing. This means that the template molecules were completely removed after three washes with ethanol.

The detective capability of MIP@CQDs was the most important step in this experiment. The fluorescence detection was carried out according to the optimum conditions. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was shown in Fig. 5A. After adding the same concentration of 4-NP, both of MIP@CQDs and NIP@CQDs had fluorescence response to 4-NP, but the quenching efficiency of MIP@CQDs was higher than that of NIP@CQDs, suggesting that MIP@CQDs had stronger selectivity for 4-NP compared with NIP@CQDs. MIP@CQDs have more binding sites suitable for 4-NP due to effective imprinting effect, which indicates that molecule-imprinted cavities with the same size and shape as template molecules are formed on the carbon quantum dots.

The fluorescence response of MIP@CQDs to different concentrations of 4-NP was tested (Fig. S5). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity was obviously weakened with the increase of 4-NP concentration. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity decreased exponentially with the increase of 4-NP concentration. Ln (F0/F) showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of 4-NP in the range of 0–144 μmol/L as shown in Fig. 5B. According to the linear equation, LOD (3σ/A) was calculated as 0.41 μmol/L, and LOQ (10σ/A) as 1.35 μmol/L. Where σ is the standard deviation of blank and A is the slope of calibration curve (n = 6). The low detection limit, high sensitivity and high correlation coefficient calculated by this method indicated that this method can be used for the detection of 4-NP in actual water samples.

Selectivity investigations were performed to further assess the performance of the as-developed sensor. A number of aromatic compounds with similar chemical structures, including 2-NP, 3-NP, NB, p-PD, AN, 4-CP, PG and HQ, were selected as potential interfering substances to assess the selectivity by comparing the values of F0/F (Liu et al., 2019). MIP@CQDs showed the greatest fluorescence quenching for 4-NP as shown in Fig. 6A, followed by 2-NP and 3-NP. The fluorescence quenching of 4-CP was greater than that of NB, p-PD, AN, PG and HQ, and the fluorescence quenching of NB, p-PD, AN, PG and HQ were very low and very close to each other. Except for 4-NP, 2-NP and 3-NP, the quenching of NIP@CQDs was low and similar to that of MIP@CQDs for the rest of the organics. The results indicated that the proposed method exhibits a good selectivity toward 4-NP. More significantly, the probe can selectively identify 4-NP from 2-NP and 3-NP. Common inorganic ions such as Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, CO3 2−, SO4 2− and NO3 −, which usually present in aqueous samples, were also investigated for the selectivity (Huang et al., 2019b; Yang et al., 2019). These common inorganic ions have no remarkable influence on the detection of 4-NP as shown in Fig. 6B. All these can be explained by the fact that the synthesized MIP@CQDs have many specific recognition sites for 4-NP, so 4-NP can be bound strongly to MIP@CQDs, leading to a decrease in luminescence intensity. The results clearly showed that MIP@CQDs are adapted to detect 4-NP in complex aqueous environments.

Fluorescence quenching may result from various mechanisms, such as internal filtration effect (IFE), fluorescence energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching or static quenching. To elucidate the feasible mechanism of MIP@CQDs fluorescence quenching by 4-NP, following systematic steps were taken as described in Scheme 2 .

The UV absorption spectra of 4-NP was overlapped strongly by both the excitation and emission spectra of MIP@CQDs (Fig. 7A), so IFE (primary and secondary) or FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) may occur. To further clarify the mechanism, each fluorescence intensity value of MIP@CQDs was corrected according to Eq. (1) in the presence of different concentrations of quencher 4-NP (Chen et al., 2018).
(1)
F corr
=
F obs
× antilog
A ex
+
A em
2
where Fobs is the observed fluorescence intensity, Fcorr is the corrected fluorescence intensity after omitting IFE from Fobs, Aex and Aem are the absorbance values of the quencher at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorophore, respectively. The suppressed efficiency (%E) was calculated for both corrected and observed fluorescence according to Eq. (2) (El-Shaheny et al., 2020).
(2)
E
%
=
1 −
F
F 0
× 100

Plots of %E of the observed and corrected fluorescence of MIP@CQDs quencher versus quencher concentration [M] (Fig. 7B) inferred that IFE is a major player in the fluorescence quenching by about 63 % of the total suppressed efficiency for 4-NP (Tarai and Mishra, 2016).

Meanwhile, there may be other mechanisms responsible for fluorescence quenching. To further explore the mechanism involved, the Stern-Volmer equation was used at 298 K, 308 K and 318 K. Corrected fluorescence excluding IFE was used in all these calculations (Zu et al., 2017).
(3)
F 0
F corr
= 1 +
K D
Q
where KD is the Stern-Volmer dynamic quenching constant and [Q] is the molar concentration of quencher. The Stern-Volmer graph of 4-NP was linear over the concentration range of 14.4–144 μM (Fig. 7C) at the three studied temperature. The slope of the Stern-Volmer plot (KD) was temperature dependent, with KD values increasing regularly with increasing temperature (Table 1 ). This behavior is a feature of dynamic quenching, as higher temperatures lead to faster diffusion and larger collision quenching (Tarai and Mishra, 2016).

As a way to define whether static quenching is involved in the quenching, the following modified Stern-Volmer equation is used to test the obtained data (Zu et al., 2017).
(4)
F 0
F corr
− 1
/ Q =
K D
+
K S
+
K D
·
K S
Q
where KS is the static quenching constant. This equation is 2 orders of magnitude in [Q] and explains the increased quenching at high [Q] when both dynamic and static quenching are included. If such relationship is valid to describe our case, a graph of [
F 0
F corr
− 1 ]/Q versus [Q] should show a linear correlation with a slope of KD · KS and intercept of KD + KS. However, it was recognized that the experimental data were not fitted to this equation and the correlation coefficient was very poor. This means that static quenching cannot be a part of the quenching mechanisms (Molina-Bolívar et al., 2016).

To demonstrate the accuracy and practicability of this method, the MIP@CQDs probe were used by standard addition method for detecting 4-NP in tap water, seawater and wastewater samples. A series of different concentrations of 4-NP were added into the water samples for fluorescence detection and the results were shown in Table 2 . No 4-NP was detected in tap water and seawater, and the concentration in the wastewater was 19.16 μmol/L. The recovery rate was 95.1 % ∼ 107.8 %. HPLC analysis was used to measure the same water samples to further verify the accuracy of the fluorescence analysis method. The parameters of HPLC method were shown in Table S1. 4-NP concentration in wastewater was 17.96 μmol/L, which was in line with the result obtained by the fluorescence analysis. The comparison strongly proved that fluorescence analysis method could be used for the analysis and detection of target pollutant accurately.

A significant amounts of research have been done for the detection of 4-NP and shown in Table S2. Our developed method provides a wide linear range with fast response time compared to other methods, and the recovery and accuracy of the method are comparable or better than other methods. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) set its maximum allowable level as 0.43 μmol/L in drinking water and the national wastewater discharge standard (GB 8978) should not exceed a maximum concentration of 3.6 μmol/L for 4-NP, both of which are above the detection limit and within the linear range of this study. The developed method is simpler, faster and more economical when compared with chromatography. Carbon quantum dots are more biocompatible than some metal quantum dots and the quenching of fluorescence was found to be caused by IFE effects and dynamic quenching. The selectivity of the fluorescence sensor was improved through the combination with MIPs and effective measurements were achieved in tap water, wastewater and seawater. The experimental results are in good agreement with the results determined by HPLC method, indicating that the method can achieve rapid determination of 4-NP in complex aqueous environments.

CQDs were prepared by one-step reaction with citric acid monohydrate as carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as surface modifier. The sol-gel method was used to prepare MIP@CQDs using 4-NP as template, CQDs as signal source, APTMS as functional monomer and TEOS as cross-linker. The prepared carbon quantum dot-imprinted fluorescence sensor has a high quenching efficiency for 4-NP. The sensor has high sensitivity, recognition ability and selectivity, and strong anti-interference ability. 4-NP quenched the fluorescence of MIP@CQDs by a combination of IFE and dynamic quenching. This probe was successfully applied to the determination of trace 4-NP in water samples, and the recoveries reached 95.1 %–107.8 %. The content of 4-NP detected in wastewater was consistent with the results of HPLC method. The results showed that the fluorescence sensor had important practical application value for detecting 4-NP in environmental samples.

Kunpeng Wang: Formal analysis, Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Liju Tan: Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yuewei Zhang: Resources, Data curation. Dongmei Zhang: Resources. Na Wang: Resources, Data curation. Jiangtao Wang: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41876078) and the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Marine Ecological Environment Science and Engineering, Ministry of Natural Resources (No. MESE-2019-06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpolbul.2023.114587.
